profile.summary: "Carl" is not a recognisable job title. At lead level, this likely means a company-specific role or a person's name rather than a standard function, so the exact scope, ownership, and partners cannot be inferred reliably from the title alone.

known_challenges.text: Unclear role boundaries can create confusion about what they own versus what others expect.
Leading without a clearly understood remit can make prioritisation and delegation harder.
Ambiguity about success measures can wear down confidence and energy.

recommended_question_themes.why: A lead with an ambiguous title needs shared understanding of ownership, decision rights, and expectations.
Clear outcomes help avoid drift and give the person a fair basis for prioritising work.
Lead-level work usually depends on getting peers and managers aligned around scope and tradeoffs.
Ambiguous leadership roles often absorb extra coordination work that can become draining.

listen_for: They spend time explaining what their role is before describing the work.
They describe repeated confusion about ownership or decision-making.
They talk more about coordination and expectation-setting than domain-specific craft.
They struggle to name clear measures of success.
avoid: Pretending the title maps cleanly to a standard role when it does not.
Judging performance against assumptions about a function you have not confirmed.
Giving advice based on invented domain jargon or responsibilities.
Treating title ambiguity as a personal failing rather than an org clarity issue.
